annotations.label: Contraction mapping

anchor.snippet: is called a contraction mapping on

annotations.note: `ContractingWith K f := K < 1 ∧ LipschitzWith K f` on an `EMetricSpace`; combined with `LipschitzWith` from `Mathlib.Topology.EMetricSpace.Lipschitz` it captures the article's `d(T(x),T(y)) ≤ q·d(x,y)` with `q < 1`.

annotations.label: Banach fixed-point theorem
annotations.note: `exists_fixedPoint` packages existence, uniqueness (via `fixedPoint_unique`) and the iteration convergence `Tendsto (fun n ↦ f^[n] x) atTop (𝓝 y)` — exactly the article's three conclusions. The unique fixed point itself is `ContractingWith.fixedPoint`.

annotations.label: A priori / a posteriori error estimates

anchors.snippet: The following inequalities are equivalent and describe the

annotations.note: Mathlib has both the a priori bound `dist (f^[n] x) (fixedPoint f hf) ≤ dist x (f x) · K^n / (1-K)` (= 2nd inequality) and the a posteriori bound `aposteriori_dist_iterate_fixedPoint_le` (= 3rd inequality); the 1st and 4th follow from `ContractingWith.dist_le_mul` iterated.

annotations.label: Lipschitz constant
annotations.note: `LipschitzWith K f := ∀ x y, edist (f x) (f y) ≤ K · edist x y`; the article's `q` is the `K` parameter.

annotations.label: Strict contraction without uniform constant: T(x)=x+1/x

anchor.snippet: is in general not enough to ensure the existence of a fixed point

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of this specific counter-example showing that `d(T(x),T(y))<d(x,y)` alone (without a uniform `q<1`) is insufficient.

annotations.label: Compact + strict contraction has a unique fixed point

anchors.snippet: if X is compact, then this weaker condition does imply the existence
In a non-empty compact metric space, any function

annotations.note: Edelstein's theorem: on a compact metric space, any `d(T x, T y) < d(x,y)` map has a unique fixed point. No matching decl in Mathlib's `Mathlib/Topology/MetricSpace/Contracting.lean` or elsewhere.

annotations.label: Application: Picard–Lindelöf theorem
annotations.note: Mathlib's Picard–Lindelöf is proved exactly via Banach's theorem applied to the integral (Picard) operator; see `ODE.picard` and `FunSpace.exists_isFixedPt_next` which use `ContractingWith.isFixedPt_fixedPoint_iterate`.

annotations.label: Lipschitz perturbations of the identity are bi-Lipschitz homeomorphisms

anchor.snippet: small Lipschitz perturbations of the identity are bi-lipschitz homeomorphisms

annotations.note: Mathlib's `ApproximatesLinearOn` framework (functions linearly approximated by an invertible CLM, in particular `I + g` with `g` Lipschitz with constant `< 1`) builds the inverse local homeomorphism; see `surjOn_closedBall_of_nonlinearRightInverse` and `toPartialHomeomorph`.

annotations.label: Application: inverse function theorem
annotations.note: The inverse function theorem in Banach spaces, proved via `ApproximatesLinearOn` (which is itself a Banach-fixed-point argument).

annotations.label: Bessaga converse to Banach's contraction principle
annotations.note: Bessaga (1959): if every iterate `f^n` of a set self-map has a unique fixed point, then for any `q ∈ (0,1)` there is a complete metric making `f` a `q`-contraction. No such metric-construction lemma exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hitzler–Seda T₁ converse (ultrametric construction)

anchor.snippet: very weak assumptions suffice to obtain such a kind of converse

annotations.note: Hitzler–Seda: for a T₁ self-map with unique fixed point that every orbit converges to, an ultrametric exists making it a `1/2`-contraction. Not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Generalization: a contracting iterate suffices
annotations.note: If `f^[n]` is `ContractingWith K`, then the (unique) fixed point of `f^[n]` is also a fixed point of `f`; combined with `fixedPoint_unique` this yields existence+uniqueness for `f` itself.

annotations.label: Generalization: summable Lipschitz constants of iterates
annotations.note: Generalization assuming `∑ c_n < ∞` where `d(T^n x, T^n y) ≤ c_n · d(x,y)`. No matching decl found in Mathlib's contracting/Lipschitz library.

annotations.label: Iterative computation of π via f(x) = x + sin x
annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize this specific numerical example (fixed-point iteration of `x ↦ x + sin x` from 3 converging to π).
